Per audit rules, leakage in a chapter-defining beat escalates to HIGH.
rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3834: rewrite Marcus's voicemail entirely. The message must convey the same information (Marcus is operating again, without middlemen, wants Carmen to call) but in Marcus's natural speech patterns — self-correcting, slightly tangled, with his characteristic 'Look' or 'What I mean is' restarts. The voicemail should sound like a man leaving a message he's rehearsed but can't quite deliver smoothly, not like a thesis statement. Example direction (do not copy): a voicemail where Marcus starts, stumbles, rephrases, and arrives at his point with the friction of real speech — maybe he says more than he means to, or catches himself and backs up. The final version should share no three-consecutive-word sequence with the calibration line.

acceptance_test: Read lines 1970-2025. (1) Carmen does something active in the scene — asks probing questions, challenges allocations, discovers information through her own reading, or makes a demand — rather than receiving a briefing. (2) The financial specifics (amounts, recipients, percentages) remain intact. (3) At least one moment of friction, disagreement, or surprise occurs during the reveal. (4) The scene does not consist of Harry speaking for more than three consecutive lines without Carmen or another character generating pressure.
problem: The financial-architecture reveal (lines 1974-2020) is structured as Harry reading from Agnes's ledger line by line while Carmen asks questions. The dramatic engine is 'lecture with Q&A' — Harry delivers figures, Carmen reacts, Harry delivers more. Carmen's role is passive audience. While the information itself is compelling (the specificity of who gets what and why is one of the novel's strengths), the scene lacks dramatic pressure, conflict, or decision. Compare with the chapter 16 confrontation, where backstory exposition is embedded in a scene with real stakes. This chapter also establishes a template — Harry-as-information-deliverer, Carmen-as-recipient — that recurs in chapters 16 and 18, making the novel's revelation architecture feel single-geared.
rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:1974-2020: restructure the financial reveal so that Carmen is an active participant rather than a passive audience. Strategy option A: Carmen discovers the financial architecture by reading Agnes's notebooks herself, with Agnes or Harry intervening only when she misreads something — this makes Carmen the investigator rather than the student. Strategy option B: embed the financial details in a scene with operational pressure — the reveal happens while the group is preparing for a handoff or dealing with a supply problem, so the numbers emerge through action rather than lecture. Strategy option C: build a disagreement into the scene — Carmen challenges the allocation (why does Marcus get 30%? why isn't more going to Burt's care?) and the financial details emerge through argument rather than presentation. Any strategy should preserve the specific dollar amounts and distribution details, which are the chapter's greatest strength.

acceptance_test: Read lines 3595-3605. (1) Carmen's discovery of the phone includes a moment of retroactive recognition — she connects the phone to something she noticed earlier. (2) The scene still creates genuine ambiguity about whether the ring is over. (3) The phone does not appear to be a device the author inserted at the last minute.
problem: Harry's second hidden phone appears at lines 3597-3599 with zero prior establishment anywhere in the manuscript. Carmen sees Harry 'holding it to his ear' — 'a cell phone she had never seen. Small, black, a flip phone like the prepaid but not the prepaid.' This phone creates the novel's key unresolved question (is the ring truly over?), but it arrives as a plot device rather than a discovered detail. The reader has spent 17 chapters in this world without any hint that Harry maintains a separate communication channel. His operational paranoia and accounting-mind redundancy are established, but no physical seed for a second phone exists. The result is that the novel's most important ambiguity — whether Harry is already rebuilding — rests on a device that feels inserted for the ending rather than woven into the story.
rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3597-3599: KEEP the second phone scene as written — it is dramatically effective. The fix belongs in earlier chapters (see companion finding for chapter 3). However, the narration at line 3597 could be tightened to make Carmen's recognition more specific: instead of 'A phone — not the prepaid... but a different one,' give Carmen a flash of retroactive recognition — something she saw earlier but didn't register, now clicking into place. This retroactive-recognition beat only works if a seed exists in an earlier chapter. If no seed is planted elsewhere, this scene will continue to read as convenient.

acceptance_test: Read lines 460-490. (1) At least one moment exists that, on rereading after chapter 17, would make a reader think 'that was the second phone' or 'that explains the second phone.' (2) The seed does not call attention to itself on first read — it passes as character texture. (3) Harry's established habits (pencil notebook, filing, contingency thinking) are not disrupted by the addition.
problem: This chapter establishes Harry's operational habits in detail — his pencil notebook (line 465), his accounting mindset, his habit of filing everything (line 477), his meticulous cash-distribution tracking (line 489). But it plants no seed for a second communication device. The prepaid phone is introduced in chapter 6 as the ring's shared burner. Harry's redundancy-thinking and contingency-planning are established, but nothing in this chapter hints that Harry might maintain a private channel the ring doesn't know about. When the second phone appears in chapter 17, there is no prior physical detail to anchor it. This is a missing-setup companion to finding F07.
rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:465-489 (the section where Harry's habits are established during the ring meeting): plant a light seed for a second device. Strategy option A: during the cash-distribution scene, Harry reaches into his cardigan pocket, touches something, and puts it back — a gesture small enough to read as habitual but specific enough that a rereader would recognize it as the second phone. Strategy option B: in Harry's interior narration about contingency and redundancy (around line 477, 'Harry filed the cough. He filed everything'), add a thought about backup systems — 'He kept his own records because he didn't trust anyone else's numbers, and he kept his own lines of communication for the same reason.' Strategy option C: during the discussion of the prepaid phone, have Harry note to himself that one phone is a single point of failure. The seed should be no more than 1-2 sentences and should not draw attention to itself on first read.

acceptance_test: Read the full chapter. (1) The chapter is between 120 and 160 lines. (2) At least one scene beat shows Tammy in her apartment experiencing it through sensation (smell, sound, temperature), not just listing its features. (3) At least one moment of care-work competence is dramatized before the surveillance begins. (4) Tammy's emotional landscape — the divorce, the financial pressure, the professional pride — emerges through scene rather than summary. (5) The chapter's voice remains flat and observational, consistent with Tammy's style-bible profile.
problem: At 63 lines (2802-2864), this is the shortest chapter in the novel and Tammy's only POV chapter. It accomplishes its informational goals — establishing Tammy's suspicion, her pattern recognition, the TV room surveillance, and her decision to watch rather than report. But the chapter sketches Tammy's emotional landscape in summary (line 2822: a single long sentence covering apartment, ex, wage) rather than inhabiting it through scene. Her wage math (line 2850) is compressed into a list. Her divorce grief is a Post-it note and a one-line reflection. Compare with Earl's chapter 15 (179 lines) or Lola's chapter 12 (165 lines), which give their POV characters room to live inside their own minds. Tammy's interiority — 'Angrier and sadder than she shows at work' per the style bible — is told rather than dramatized. This is her first and last chance to be a POV character, and 63 lines don't give her enough room to earn it.
rewrite_direction: Expand to approximately 120-150 lines. At snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:2822: break the summary sentence into a scene — show Tammy arriving at the apartment, experiencing it (the cat smell, the Christian radio from Grace downstairs, the hook Kevin installed, the stained couch), rather than listing its features. At line 2850: expand the wage math into a scene beat — Tammy at the kitchen table or in bed, running the numbers the way Carmen does in chapter 7, with the same grinding specificity. Add at least one scene moment before the parking-lot surveillance where Tammy's competence is visible through her care work — she notices something about a resident's skin or a medication timing that nobody else catches. This grounds her observation skills in demonstrated expertise, not just claimed experience. Keep the chapter's tight observational style — Tammy's voice should remain flat and cataloguing — but give her enough room to be a person, not just a surveillance function.

acceptance_test: Read lines 3778-3785. (1) Carmen's response to Harry's 'You did right by us' is no more than two sentences. (2) The response contains no summary catalog of prior events. (3) At least one concrete sensory detail grounds the moment in the body or the room. (4) The moral ambiguity — whether she did right — remains unresolved. (5) The passage trusts the reader to carry the accumulated weight of prior chapters without being reminded of them.
problem: At lines 3780-3782, after Harry says 'You did right by us,' Carmen's internal response catalogs her transgressions in summary list form: 'She'd lied to a state official, hidden a dictionary full of pills, flushed controlled substances down a handicapped toilet in small batches at three in the morning, burned a pharmacist's meticulous records behind a loading dock. She'd watched Burt decline and told herself his prescriptions were helping people. She'd said "us" and meant it.' The reader witnessed every one of these events in earlier chapters. This is the novel telling the reader what to feel at the exact moment it should trust the reader to feel it. The list converts earned dramatic weight into moral inventory — it explicates rather than resonates.
rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_02/FINAL_NOVEL.md:3782: replace the catalog with a single, concrete, sensory response. Strategy option A: Carmen's reaction is physical — her hands still smell like smoke, or she sees the pen-dent marks on the card table where Agnes's notebooks used to sit. One specific detail that synecdochically carries the weight of everything she's done. Strategy option B: Carmen simply doesn't answer, and the narration stays in her body — she looks at Harry, she looks at her hands, she leaves. The silence IS the answer. Strategy option C: keep one item from the list — the single thing that weighs most on her — and cut the rest. The passage should be no more than two sentences. The rule: if the reader has already watched Carmen do it, the novel does not need to remind them.
